I led the LATAM team at Microsoft in 2015.
I have led teams across five continents.
We deployed the framework last quarter.
I have carried that lesson into every role since.
Thanks for making the time, Lucia. I am David, a recruiter for the AI Safety leadership team here. This is a senior role, so let me start simple: walk me through your background.
Of course. I have spent almost forty years in technology, and I have led teams on three continents. Most recently, I moved into AI safety, and I have owned that work ever since.
Good. Give me one concrete example. Tell me about a time you led a major transformation.

In 2015, I led a difficult migration at a large company. We deployed a new security framework across four countries, and we delivered every outcome on time. Since then, I have carried that discipline into every project.
I like that. Here is what I care about most in this role: accountability. When something breaks, who owns it?
I do. I take full ownership. Over the years, I have learned that a leader who shares the credit must also carry the blame. That is how I have built trust with every stakeholder.
That is exactly what we look for. One more thing: why is your past experience relevant to a role that did not exist five years ago?
Because the tools change, but the judgment carries over. I have managed risk and aligned people my whole career, and that is precisely what AI safety needs today.
In 2015, I led a major transformation across four countries.
Since then, I have owned every project I touch.
I take full accountability for the outcome.
My experience carries over into everything I do today.
Let me tell you what I did, and why it still matters.

Let me tell you what I listen for when I interview a senior leader. Two things, really. First, the facts. What did you actually do? I want the past simple here. You led a team, you shipped a product, you closed a deal in a specific year. That is your record, and it needs to be clear. But facts alone do not win the offer. The second thing is the bridge. Why does that experience still matter, here, today, for this role? That is where the present perfect does the work. When a candidate says, I have carried that lesson into every project since, I lean in. Do not just list what you did ten years ago. Show me why it still lives in how you work now.

So, when they ask me to walk through my background, I try to do two things at once. I start with the facts, in the past. I joined the company in 2015. I led the security team through a very hard migration that year. We deployed a new framework across four countries, and we hit every deadline. Those are finished events, so I use the past simple. But then I connect it to now. Since that project, I have owned every rollout I touch. I have built a real track record of shipping on time, and I have never lost that sense of accountability. The past tells them what happened. The present perfect tells them who I am today. That is the difference between an archive and an argument.

In 2015, I joined the company as a security lead. That year, I led a difficult migration across four countries. We deployed the new framework and delivered every outcome on time. Since that project, I have owned every rollout I touch. And I have carried that sense of accountability into every role since.
